chunk_summary: The PM POSHAN scheme in India provides school meals with specified food group quantities to improve nutrition and deliver fortified inputs, while serving as a platform for health interventions like deworming and anemia management.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional standards and fortified inputs in PM POSHAN school meals.
Identify the role of schools in delivering pediatric health interventions like IFA supplementation and deworming.
Assess the impact of fortified foods on micronutrient adequacy and pediatric health outcomes.
Recognize the importance of monitoring school meal consumption and addressing related challenges.
key_facts: PM POSHAN meals provide 450 kcal and 12 g protein for primary classes, and 700 kcal and 20 g protein for upper primary classes.
Food group quantities per school day include 100-150 g foodgrain, 20-30 g pulses, 50-75 g vegetables, and 5-7.5 g oil.
Fortified inputs include fortified rice/atta, iodized or double fortified salt, and fortified oil.
Weekly IFA supplementation and biannual deworming are key health interventions delivered through schools.
Children with iron deficiency anemia require therapeutic iron beyond fortified meals.
Monitoring meal consumption and addressing stigma or illness-related meal skipping is critical.
reference_quote: Operational guidelines specify per-child food-group quantities that translate into macro targets: for primary classes, roughly 100 g foodgrain, 20 g pulses, 50 g vegetables, and 5 g oil per school day (about 450 kcal and 12 g protein).
document_key_topics: PM POSHAN school meal norms and nutritional standards
Fortified food inputs in school meals
